comment_text: stopped it while watching the 4th episode ... i had had enough of that weirdo, ed gein.  damn you to hell, ed gein, you were a big time WEIRDO with your lil' girl voice ! (if he really talked like that)  :)  Plus the whole subplots about hitchcock, anthony perkins, the texas chainsaw massacre, etc.  not interesting, waste of time ... and those were huge parts of the episodes.   i watched 20 minutes of episode 4 and then i thought .... damn, this is so boring, repetitive and confusing ... and i turned it off (!)   also it's often so dark on the screen, you can't even see anything. With all the changes, going back in time, going forward, hallucinations, characters that don't exist, trying to figure what is true and what is just his imagination ...  you don't even know what is going on half the time .... way too long for its own good, drop all the fake subplots and make it just 2-3 episodes, it would have been more watchable.

comment_text: I have a good friend who use to work for Libbys in Rochester, Mn. He drove the harvester tractors when the peas and corn were ready. He was sent to a field in Wisconsin to harvest peas and it turned out to be some of the old Gein property. He said the entire time he was there he had the creeps. He couldn't explain it at first, but later told a friend at work, and thats when they told him about Ed. He had never heard the story, but that didn't stop him from being freaked out. After that he requested jobs for anywhere else but Wisconsin.

comment_text:
Just not into taking liberties about real people. The misogyny and ageism is apparent. Both Ed and Henry were abused horribly by their alcoholic father (though people blaming women, don’t they).  That trauma can definitely trigger schizophrenia and with an isolated existence and lack of understanding/ anti-sexist education, certain things contribute. Patriarchy allowing men to abuse and torture people definitely allows for this sort of thing. And blaming /framing Adeline (who is actually around the same age as Gein) for simply talking to him is also horrific to her memory. He also killed two middle aged hard working independent women. No young women like the movie portrays. Because it may be sexual in nature (though I think it’s mostly schizophrenia and growing up on a farm with an abusive father and seeing how he treated women), I guess Hollywood can’t fathom someone targeting middle aged women even though that’s actually more common. 🤷🏻‍♀️🤦🏻🙄. It’s often abusive alcoholic sexist fathers who make serial killers.

The ending makes me think his abusive dad also raped her in front of them. While Ed was never accused or suspected of rape, it’s a form of violence. Violence against women.  That possibly conflicted with what his mom taught him (to never hurt women), and so he hurt them in a less common way (though actually it’s pretty common to murder women).

comment_text:
This is probably going to be unpopular but I think fictionalising things to the point where he seems like an innocent victim who wanted to do good for the world in the end (by helping to catch ted bundy, wich didn't happen at all in real life) is doing the world a disservice. 
I know that it's a tv show but I just think that when you use real life people, you have a responsability to the victims. And I think it was incredibly disrespectful to the victims by making bernice seem like a sex obsessed crazy person who didn't want to take no for an awnser and even calling her the town bicycle, when in real life there is absolutely no indication of this at all, not even that they went on a date. And they used her actual name. Same goes with the real life girl he allegedly dated, they make her out to be not only an accomplice but even an instigator on a some of the gruesome acts Gein actualy did, when again there is absolutely no indication of this in real life. They make him more sympathetic whilst making the victims seem a little less innocent and this causes a lot of people to disconnect from reality and sometimes even idolize an actual killer. Same happened with dahmer, a lot of people saying that they thought he was cute and felt bad for him, when he killed, dismembered and ate over 17 people (some of them children).

comment_text: When I was a child my uncle was in Mendota. My mom would take us with her to visit him to bring him tobacco   and rolling papers, candy, and other treats. I saw Ed Gein in the day room. I went back to my elementary school class and told everyone I saw him and my teacher actually asked my mom if it was true, lol. (She lived on our street) My mom was upset because she didn't want anyone to know that my uncle was there. Recently she gave me a book about Ed Gein written long ago by someone who worked the case. It was signed by Ed himself! (I actually have a photo of him signing the same book, but I don't know how many he signed or if that was the actual copy!! Pretty cool. Or pretty creepy. For you to decide.

comment_text: Fascinating case that inspired several horror films as well as other serial killers. However, some fictional parts reviewers have noted IMO went too far beyond creative license. That and blatantly gratuitous scenes for unneeded shock value ultimately made me not want to watch any more from the show creators. I did appreciate that they also showed Gein was timid and compliant growing up isolated at home and was abused by his parents for many years, and it showed his struggles with real mental illness. He was a very sick man with the mind of a child, in a time and place that were not conducive to him getting a mental health diagnosis and treatment, which led him to do horrible acts. From all witness accounts, ironically, he seemed to be well liked otherwise.

video_title: MONSTER: The Ed Gein Story | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I just watched the trailer for Monster Season 3: Ed Gein Story, and it’s honestly full of cinematic references that connect perfectly to classic horror history. The woman suit Ed wears looks almost identical to Norman Bates’s outfit from Psycho — that same eerie, handmade clothing that instantly brings to mind the infamous shower scene. And just like in Psycho, there’s a scene where Ed kills a girl in the shower while wearing that costume, which feels like a direct homage to Hitchcock’s film.

I also noticed subtle references to World War II throughout the show — the tone, the setting, and certain visuals give off that post-war dread, as if the series is showing how the horrors of war twisted the small-town psyche of that era. It adds a haunting layer of realism.

The show even borrows from Ed Gein: The Butcher of Plainfield (2007) — especially that part where Ed kidnaps a girl, which never actually happened in real life. It’s clear the creators are blending fact and fiction, mixing real history with the myth built by horror movies over the decades.

Charlie Hunnam’s portrayal of Ed is fascinating too. His voice sounds surprisingly close to Steve Railsback’s version, probably because both went for that deep, calm, unsettling tone that captures how quiet yet unnerving the real Ed Gein was.

And the Easter eggs don’t stop there — there’s a scene where Ed chases two hunters through the woods with a chainsaw, almost identical to Leatherface chasing Sally Hardesty at the end of The Texas Chainsaw Massacre (1974). Even the camera movement feels like a tribute to that final chase.

Then there’s another chilling moment: Ed dancing alone in his house, wearing his woman suit — exactly like Buffalo Bill from The Silence of the Lambs when he dances in his basement, lost in his delusion. It’s creepy, tragic, and strangely symbolic of how those horror characters were all inspired by the same real-life figure — Ed himself.

What’s crazy is that Ed in Monster Season 3 actually looks like Scout from Team Fortress 2! Same build, same facial structure, same energy — it’s like Scout took a dark turn and started a “home decor” hobby. 😂

So now we’ve got Ed Gein who dresses like Norman Bates, chases people like Leatherface, dances like Buffalo Bill, sounds like Steve Railsback, and looks like Scout from TF2. That’s literally the most cursed crossover in horror history — and I love it.

Monster Season 3: Ed Gein Story looks absolutely insane — a mix of biography, cinematic tribute, and psychological horror. It’s disturbing, stylish, and full of hidden details. I can’t wait to watch it.

comment_text:
I hate the idea of people idolizing / glorifying killers, but I loved Dahmer because it's so interesting to learn and study the psychology of these murderers. 

I love analyzing and seeing how their mental state and minds can even comprehend such actions. 

Hopefully we get a series based on the World Fair Butcher also, H.H Homes. He's one of the interesting ones to study due to him being America's Jack the Ripper, and the first Serial Killer in the US also.

video_title: MONSTER: The Ed Gein Story | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: The issue with this show is that Ed Gein, even as a serial killer, was very reclusive and hardly revealed much about himself, his intentions and his killings. With Dahmer, more was known, there was more public knowledge and so the creatives didn't have to use their imagination as much. It was a realier, more brooding and grittier take. This edges into fantasy and the Alfred Hitchcock stuff is just schlocky. It's not a good show, which is a shame because Charlie is quite good in it.

video_title: MONSTER: The Ed Gein Story | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: I'm quite familiar with Ed Gein.  I have Harold Schechter's book on Ed Gein.  Artistic license aside, Ed didn't sound like the way the actor portrayed -- just kind of FYI for those interested, but I think the odd voice is a method of conveying him as an "oddball", not an attempt to mirror his actual voice.  You can hear Ed Gein's voice on interview tapes via the docuseries, "Psycho:  The Lost Tapes of Ed Gein".  Much of the storyline in the Netflix series is made up.  For example, there was no girl that he was dating that found Bernice Worden's body.  Police found her when they entered the shed.  Not a bad Netflix show, but largely "artistic", with some facts sprinkled throughout the show.  If you really want the raw facts, read Harold Schechter's book, "Deviant".  If you found that interesting, get his book called "Fiend" - it's another true crime novel he wrote, about Jesse Pomeroy.  Though I have yet to read it, "Bestial" is one of his acclaimed books as well.

comment_text:
Bloody hell! Not an easy watch. It was only towards the end when the doctor explained that it was an illness that made him do what he did. That most of his experiences were only in his own head. Like Bernice Worden giving him her underwear, him killing Mary Hogan in a bar full of celebrating Nazis, him being chased down by escaped concentration camp prisoners while his Mother is asking him what he's doing and Adaline having no problem with him killing people!
